Name: curvature-aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing via Deterministic Spectral Proxies
Short Hypothesis: The local curvature of the loss landscape can serve as a deterministic proxy for precision allocation in mixed-precision inference, enabling training-free, latency-aware routing through spectral decomposition of activation covariances.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision methods rely on static profiling or heuristic calibration, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions. Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) introduced a training-free framework based on activation geometry but lacks explicit curvature-awareness. Our proposal builds on SQR by incorporating curvature diagnostics via Hessian-vector products, providing a more nuanced precision allocation mechanism. This differs from SQR by leveraging curvature information to better capture sensitivity to quantization noise.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference offers significant latency and memory gains but typically requires costly calibration or static profiling. We propose Curvature-Aware Precision Routing (CAPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on the local curvature of the loss landscape. CAPR computes Hessian-vector products on-the-fly using randomized numerical linear algebra, mapping curvature diagnostics to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link curvature to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, CAPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, CAPR provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate CAPR on WILDS benchmarks, measuring throughput and accuracy under natural distribution shifts.
Compare CAPR to static mixed-precision baselines and SQR, using Pareto frontier analysis.
Conduct ablation studies to isolate the impact of curvature diagnostics on precision allocation.
Measure the computational overhead of Hessian-vector product computations and analyze their scalability.
Validate theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds with empirical generalization stability under quantization noise.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Computational overhead of Hessian-vector products may limit scalability for very large models.
Theoretical bounds may not fully capture the complexity of real-world distribution shifts.
Precision allocation decisions may be suboptimal for highly non-convex loss landscapes.
Empirical validation is limited to single-GPU setups, and results may not generalize to multi-GPU environments.
